transcript_v1.text: Hello everyone. By a quick show of hands, how many of you here either work or know someone who works in oil and gas?
It's quite a few, right? And we all know how important oil and gas is to Calgary. For decades it has brought in hundreds of billions in economic development.
And there are companies that are headquartered here that operate assets that are worth billions all across the world.
Now, whether those assets rely on me depends heavily on one activity, inspections.
So these inspections, they determine whether equipment stays in service, gets repaired, or gets shut down.
And you may imagine, if something goes wrong here, we're not only talking about a lot of money, but also about people's safety.
Now here's the contradiction. You have a modern facility that runs your blood count sensors, real-time monitoring, predictive analysis.
But the moment an inspection finishes, the process checks back decades.
You have a highly trained professional evaluating complex equipment, and then spending hours to manually convert what you already know into real work.
And here's the real problem. You see, these inspectors are trained to make reliability decisions.
So it is not only a waste, but an underutilization of their skill to have them spend hours on reporting.
And no matter how good your internal standards and procedures are, there's always going to be some variation.
Because at the end of the day, every inspector sees and frames the situation differently.
The real problem, that variation, is a problem for the reliability and data analytics for these programs.
Because as soon as your data starts becoming bearable, the decision quality becomes very poor.
We built Inspecto to change that. Inspecto is an app that lets users take pictures, report voice notes, and observations on the go.
It then automatically gathers all of that information, understands the specific inspection context, and builds the structured report in real time.
Inspecto aims to reduce data variability, information waiting to be processed, and ultimately lead times.
For inspectors, this means that now hours of reporting becomes just minutes of reviewing.
For managers, it means better decision making, it means less report, and better data quality.
And for companies, ultimately, this means that they can now run more efficient reliability programs and be more regulatory compliant.
Because whenever an inspector documents differently, your integrity program is only as strong as your weakest report.
Thank you very much.
Thank you.

transcript_vfinal.text: Hello everyone. Quick show of hands: how many of you work in oil and gas, or know a friend, family member, or colleague who does?
That’s a lot of hands, right? And in Calgary, that makes sense. For decades, oil and gas has created hundreds of billions in economic development.
Some of the companies headquartered here operate assets worth billions of dollars across the world.
And the reliability of those assets often comes down to one thing: inspections.
Those inspections decide whether equipment stays in service, gets repaired, or gets shut down.
So if something goes wrong here, the stakes are not just financial. You are also talking about people’s safety.
Now here’s the contradiction. You can walk through a modern facility with sensors everywhere, real-time monitoring, and predictive analytics.
But the moment an inspection ends, the workflow snaps back decades.
A highly trained professional evaluates complex equipment, then spends hours manually turning that insight into a report.
And that is the real problem. Inspectors are trained to make reliability decisions.
Their value is in judgment, not in spending hours on reporting.
And no matter how strong your standards and procedures are, some variation always gets in.
Because at the end of the day, every inspector sees the situation a little differently and frames it differently.
And that variation does real damage to both reliability programs and the data analytics behind them.
When your data is inconsistent, decision quality drops fast.
That’s why we built Inspecto. It’s an app that lets inspectors capture photos, voice notes, and observations while they work.
It then brings all of that together, understands the inspection context, and builds a structured report in real time.
Inspecto is designed to reduce data variability, reduce information waiting to be processed, and ultimately shorten lead times.
For inspectors, that means hours of reporting become minutes of review.
For managers, it means better decision-making, less reporting burden, and better data quality.
And for companies, ultimately, it means more efficient reliability programs and stronger regulatory compliance.
Because whenever inspectors document differently, your integrity program is only as strong as its weakest report.
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
